text:
for enterprises across industries including finance and banking, retail, and media.
GreenNode expands the HAN Region with new Availability Zone HAN-1B
Previously, GreenNode’s Hanoi region operated with a single availability zone, HAN-1A. With the launch of HAN-1B, the HAN Region now consists of two independent Availability Zones.
This architecture enables customers to deploy systems using a multi-AZ cloud architecture, improving application resilience and fault tolerance. It also helps enterprises meet data storage and protection compliance requirements under Vietnamese regulations such as the Data Law 2024 and the Personal Data Protection Law 2025.
Following the addition of Availability Zone HAN-1B, GreenNode’s infrastructure now includes:
HAN Region (Hanoi): 2 Availability Zones
HCM Region (Ho Chi Minh City): 3 Availability Zones
BKK Region (Bangkok): 1 Availability Zone
For industries with strict security and compliance requirements, such as finance and banking, where transaction systems and customer data must operate 24/7, a multi-region and multi-Availability Zone cloud architecture plays a critical role in ensuring both system availability and regulatory compliance.

title: High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
text:
High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
Key takeaways
GreenNode has introduced new high-performance Compute Flavors in early 2026, designed as the core infrastructure layer for AI-native enterprises.
These instances combine powerful CPU options, NVIDIA GPU support, fast networking, and optimized storage to handle large-scale AI training, inference, and data-intensive workloads reliably.
The goal is to give AI-native businesses ready-to-use, scalable cloud instances so they can focus on building models and products instead of managing complex infrastructure.
In the era of artificial intelligence, compute performance is no longer just a technical specification. It has become the core foundation that determines model training speed, system scalability, and a company’s competitive advantage. At GreenNode, performance is not an add-on feature — it is a guiding design principle embedded throughout our entire infrastructure architecture.

title: High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
text:
age. At GreenNode, performance is not an add-on feature — it is a guiding design principle embedded throughout our entire infrastructure architecture.
Continuing this vision, GreenNode officially launched its new high-performance Compute Flavors in early 2026. This upgrade not only expands the range of configurations available, but also reaffirms our commitment to delivering a high-performance computing platform optimized for enterprises entering the AI-native era.
Diverse configurations – Optimized for every workload
Rather than focusing on virtual quantity, GreenNode prioritizes delivering a practical and well-optimized range of configurations tailored to real enterprise workloads. Similar to hyperscale cloud providers, GreenNode’s compute flavors are logically categorized into four main groups, powered by high-performance processors from Intel and AMD:
General Purpose: Balanced CPU and memory resources, suitable for most web servers and enterprise applications.
Standard: Optimized for stable performance across common workloads.
High Memory: Designed for applications requiring large RAM capacity, such as in-memory databases or big data analytics.

title: High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
text:
mance across common workloads.
High Memory: Designed for applications requiring large RAM capacity, such as in-memory databases or big data analytics.
High CPU: Built for compute-intensive workloads that demand strong processing power and fast data handling.
High-performance cloud instances with high-speed networking
A key highlight of this latest upgrade is the introduction of Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P processors. This marks a significant step forward in realizing our vision of delivering high-performance AI cloud infrastructure for digital enterprises.
To meet demanding requirements for data transfer speed, GreenNode offers two network bandwidth options:
Network 1GBps: Available across a wide range of configurations, from entry-level options like s2-general-1x1 to high-capacity instances such as s2-highmem-32x256.
Network 10GBps: Designed for latency-sensitive and bandwidth-intensive workloads, including configurations like s2-standard-64x256-n10 and s2-highcpu-32x64-n10.

With a 1 Gbps network, GreenNode provides a wide range of configurations from entry-level to high-end, supporting workloads from standard application operations to large-scale enterprise systems. Meanwhile, flavors with the “-n10” suffix are equipped with 10 Gbps bandwidth, making them particularly suitable for AI training, Big Data pipelines, and real-time systems that require high throughput.
The flexibility to choose between 1 Gbps and 10 Gbps networking enables enterprises to optimize costs while ensuring performance that aligns with each stage of their growth.
Why GreenNode is a top choice for AI-native enterprises
With its upgraded infrastructure architecture, GreenNode goes beyond simply providing compute resources. It builds an AI-ready ecosystem from the ground up, where every component is optimized to help enterprises deploy faster, operate more efficiently, and maintain better long-term cost control.

title: High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
text:
ground up, where every component is optimized to help enterprises deploy faster, operate more efficiently, and maintain better long-term cost control.
Optimized Total Cost of Ownership (TCO): Reduce long-term infrastructure investment and operational costs while maximizing resource efficiency.
Superior price-to-performance: Deliver greater computing capability per budget unit, ensuring strong performance relative to investment.
Local regulatory compliance: Ensure data security and full compliance with Vietnamese regulations such as the Data Law 2024 and the Personal Data Protection Law 2025.
From infrastructure to AI applications in just a few clicks
Beyond the high-performance Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P CPU platform, GreenNode also provides a range of specialized GPUs designed for advanced AI and Machine Learning workloads.

title: High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
text:
e Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P CPU platform, GreenNode also provides a range of specialized GPUs designed for advanced AI and Machine Learning workloads.
GreenNode’s GPU Cloud integrates multiple high-performance NVIDIA GPUs, including NVIDIA H100 Tensor Core GPU, L40S, A40, RTX 5090, and RTX 4090. This diverse portfolio enables enterprises to flexibly choose configurations suitable for each stage of model development — from model tuning, LLM training, and deep learning processing to real-time inferencing and enterprise AI applications.
In addition, GreenNode cloud servers are deployed on international-standard data center infrastructure, operating under a Multi-Availability Zone (Multi-AZ) architecture to ensure high availability and stability. Currently, GreenNode operates 03 regions: HCM - Ho Chi Minh City (3 AZs), HAN - Hanoi (2 AZs) và BKK – Bangkok (1 AZ).
The infrastructure meets international standards, allowing customers to easily deploy a multi-AZ architecture similar to a hyperscaler to increase system availability.

title: High-performance Cloud Instances – A solid foundation for AI-native enterprises
text:
e meets international standards, allowing customers to easily deploy a multi-AZ architecture similar to a hyperscaler to increase system availability.
This architecture allows enterprises to flexibly scale VM resources based on real workload demands, maintain stable performance for critical AI workloads, and optimize costs through a flexible usage model.
With the combination of high-performance CPUs and specialized GPU systems, GreenNode delivers a comprehensive AI platform — ready from infrastructure to application — providing a strong foundation for a wide range of AI workloads:
LLM Training: Supporting the development and fine-tuning of large-scale AI models.
Model Inference Deployment: Running AI models in production environments with real-time processing capabilities.
AI Solution Development: Building chatbots and intelligent enterprise applications.
Everything is ready for enterprises to start their AI journey in just a few steps of server initialization. Experience the GreenNode infrastructure today and discover the difference of a high-performance AI cloud platform built for AI, ML, and Big Data workloads.

title: GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
text:
GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
Key takeaways
GreenNode’s AI cloud runs on STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City 1, a Tier III, ISO-certified, PCI-DSS-compliant data center with 9.6 MW IT load, 1,600 racks, and carrier-neutral connectivity to major telcos and hyperscalers.
The partnership with STT GDC Vietnam gives customers a modern, secure, and highly reliable infrastructure platform, minimizing downtime and supporting demanding workloads from banks, fintech, gaming, cloud, and enterprise segments.
With direct peering and cloud-on-ramps plus DCIM-based monitoring, GreenNode can deliver stable low-latency AI cloud services in Vietnam while meeting strict global security and compliance standards.
ST Telemedia Global Data Centres (STT GDC) is one of the world’s fastest-growing data centre providers. STT GDC Vietnam meets the Uptime Tier III standards in both the design and construction of its equipment infrastructure for DCs at the STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City 1 (previously referred to as VNG Data Center)
In response to the ever-evolving needs of Vietnamese businesses

title: GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
text:
of-the-art data centers in Vietnam to achieve Uptime Tier III certification for both design (TCDD) and installation equipment for data centers (TCCF).
It is also ISO 27001, ISO 27017, ISO 27018 and PCI-DSS certified, offering a total IT load capacity of 9.6 megawatts.  It is designed to meet the global standards of local and international banks, financial institutions, Internet exchange operators, local, regional and international network service providers, content delivery network providers, edge computing cloud providers and other enterprise segments. Strategically positioned as Ho Chi Minh City's first carrier-neutral connectivity hub, it provides network coverage from major local telecommunication operators and services including cloud access to leading providers, local leased lines and disaster recovery workspace. Customers in STT GDC will be able to establish a direct peering connection to VNGGames and VNG Cloud.

title: GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
text:
al leased lines and disaster recovery workspace. Customers in STT GDC will be able to establish a direct peering connection to VNGGames and VNG Cloud.
The Uptime Tier III certificate is granted by the Uptime Institute (USA), the world's leading and prestigious assessment standard system. A team of experienced experts will provide accurate and professional evaluations of the level of "precision" in the process of designing, constructing, operating, and managing the data center.
In the context of strong digital transformation, minimizing downtime to the maximum extent helps businesses control operating costs and optimize profits.
“We believe our partnership with STT GDC, a globally recognised leader in data center operations, will set new service standards worldwide for Vietnam's data center industry as well as introduce our products and services to international markets. Together, we are advancing the AI Cloud movement to support both regional and local businesses, in line with key trends in the digital economy,” stated Le Hong Minh, Founder & CEO of VNG Group.
Read more: VNG and STT GDC Joint Venture Announcement
Modern, secure, and highly reliable data center in Vietnam

title: GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
text:
ong Minh, Founder & CEO of VNG Group.
Read more: VNG and STT GDC Joint Venture Announcement
Modern, secure, and highly reliable data center in Vietnam
STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City covers an area of 7,800 square meters with a usable floor area of 3,100 square meters. It has the capacity to accommodate 1,600 server racks, with a design capacity of up to 10kW per rack. It provides a place where businesses can confidently host their servers with high levels of safety and security.
To achieve Tier III Certification Constructed Facility (TCCF) for data center, all equipment and machinery at the DC are carefully selected and imported from renowned manufacturers worldwide. The cooling system is modern and highly precise, with the ability to accurately control temperatures in each area, resulting in energy efficiency. STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City has an N+1 network system for both domestic and international connections, ensuring stable, secure, and highly reliable internet connectivity, along with easy connectivity to all network providers. STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City also has dedicated connections with the leading cloud computing providers, namely AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

title: GreenNode’s Infrastructure Backed by Partnership with STT GDC Vietnam
text:
rk providers. STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City also has dedicated connections with the leading cloud computing providers, namely AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.
The entire essential infrastructure at STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City is managed and monitored through a dedicated infrastructure management system called DCIM (Data Center Infrastructure Management) tool. This system enables the on-site infrastructure management team to have detailed monitoring of all components throughout the data center, including power, cooling, network equipment, servers, and telecommunications connections.
STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City ensures businesses a secure digital infrastructure platform that meets international security criteria. This helps businesses save costs, focus on innovation activities, improve customer experience, increase revenue, and expand their scale.

of-the-art data centers in Vietnam to achieve Uptime Tier III certification for both design (TCDD) and installation equipment for data centers (TCCF).

Modern, secure, and highly reliable data center in Vietnam
STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City covers an area of 7,800 square meters with a usable floor area of 3,100 square meters. It has the capacity to accommodate 1,600 server racks, with a design capacity of up to 10kW per rack. It provides a place where businesses can confidently host their servers with high levels of safety and security.
To achieve Tier III Certification Constructed Facility (TCCF) for data center, all equipment and machinery at the DC are carefully selected and imported from renowned manufacturers worldwide. The cooling system is modern and highly precise, with the ability to accurately control temperatures in each area, resulting in energy efficiency. STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City has an N+1 network system for both domestic and international connections, ensuring stable, secure, and highly reliable internet connectivity, along with easy connectivity to all network providers. STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City also has dedicated connections with the leading cloud computing providers, namely AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

rk providers. STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City also has dedicated connections with the leading cloud computing providers, namely AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.
The entire essential infrastructure at STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City is managed and monitored through a dedicated infrastructure management system called DCIM (Data Center Infrastructure Management) tool. This system enables the on-site infrastructure management team to have detailed monitoring of all components throughout the data center, including power, cooling, network equipment, servers, and telecommunications connections.
STT VNG Ho Chi Minh City ensures businesses a secure digital infrastructure platform that meets international security criteria. This helps businesses save costs, focus on innovation activities, improve customer experience, increase revenue, and expand their scale.

How GreenNode Supports Regional Workloads Across Vietnam and Southeast Asia
Key takeaways
GreenNode operates AI-optimized cloud infrastructure in both Vietnam and Thailand, giving enterprises a regional foundation to run low-latency, production workloads across Southeast Asia.
The platform addresses four key constraints for companies entering Vietnam—latency from offshore hosting, regulatory and data localization requirements, GPU scarcity, and multi-country scalability—by providing onshore, GPU-ready, and regionally distributed infrastructure.
GreenNode is designed for hybrid cloud, integrating smoothly with global hyperscalers while offering compliant, high-performance environments for AI-driven, fintech, gaming, e-commerce, and other digital workloads that need reliability, redundancy, and predictable performance at scale.
As Vietnam’s digital economy continues to expand, cloud infrastructure requirements are becoming more demanding. According to multiple industry forecasts, Vietnam’s cloud market is maintaining double-digit annual growth, driven by AI adoption, fintech expansion, e-commerce scale, and digital transformation across enterprises.

Latency from Offshore Hosting
Many global companies initially serve Vietnamese users from Singapore. While geographically close, cross-border routing can introduce measurable latency, particularly during peak hours or for real-time applications such as gaming, AI inference APIs, fintech transactions, and media streaming.

Regulatory and Data Sensitivity

Onshore infrastructure reduces this uncertainty and simplifies engagement with local partners and regulators.

With a population of over 100 million, fast-growing internet penetration, and strong government support for digital transformation, the country is attracting increasing attention from foreign SaaS companies, AI startups, fintech platforms, gaming studios, and regional enterprises.
However, entering Vietnam is not simply a matter of launching servers in the nearest hyperscale region. Infrastructure decisions now directly influence regulatory compliance, latency, user experience, and long-term scalability.
In 2026, most foreign companies expanding into Vietnam adopt a hybrid cloud strategy. Rather than replacing their global cloud providers, they extend them with a local infrastructure layer designed to handle Vietnam-specific requirements. The success of this model depends heavily on choosing the right regional cloud partner.
Why Hybrid Cloud Is Becoming the Default Model in Vietnam

Why Hybrid Cloud Is Becoming the Default Model in Vietnam
Foreign companies entering Vietnam often already operate on platforms such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud. These hyperscalers power core global systems including identity management, analytics, CI/CD pipelines, and multi-region deployments.
At first glance, it may seem efficient to serve Vietnamese users from nearby regions such as Singapore. However, several structural realities make this approach less ideal over time.
Latency and User Experience
While Singapore is geographically close, cross-border routing can introduce measurable latency. For consumer-facing applications such as gaming, fintech payments, media streaming, and real-time analytics dashboards, milliseconds matter.
For AI-powered applications, particularly inference APIs that serve local users, network latency directly affects perceived product quality.
Data Localization and Regulatory Sensitivity

Vietnam’s regulatory environment continues to emphasize data localization, particularly for industries that process user data such as fintech, e-commerce, gaming, and digital platforms. While requirements vary by sector, hosting sensitive workloads offshore can introduce uncertainty and compliance complexity.
For foreign companies seeking long-term presence, aligning infrastructure with local expectations reduces risk and simplifies regulatory dialogue.

Relying solely on offshore GPU regions can introduce cost volatility, resource scarcity, and data transfer overhead.

What a Modern Hybrid Cloud Architecture Looks Like
For foreign companies expanding into Vietnam in 2026, a typical architecture includes:
Global Layer: Core infrastructure remains on hyperscalers. This includes global services, authentication, internal tooling, centralized analytics, and multi-region failover.
Vietnam Layer: Latency-sensitive APIs, AI inference endpoints, user data storage, and regulated workloads are deployed locally within Vietnam.
Regional Layer (Optional): Infrastructure in a neighboring Southeast Asian country supports redundancy, regional scaling, or disaster recovery.
This model allows companies to preserve global consistency while optimizing performance and compliance at the local level.
The key question becomes: which local cloud provider can support this architecture effectively?

Why Choosing the Right Cloud Provider in Vietnam Matters in 2026
Vietnam is entering a new phase of digital growth. By 2026, the country is no longer viewed only as a manufacturing hub or an outsourcing destination. For foreign companies planning to enter or expand in Vietnam, cloud infrastructure is no longer a background technical decision. It is a core part of market entry strategy.

Vietnam Cloud Market Overview 2026
Vietnam’s cloud market in 2026 represents a mature yet fast-growing opportunity for both global and regional providers. For foreign companies planning to enter or expand in Vietnam, the landscape is increasingly dynamic and competitive, shaped by three key forces:
Strong and sustained market growth
a growing emphasis on local infrastructure and regulatory compliance

Rapid Market Growth and Expansion
Vietnam’s cloud computing market is expanding quickly, with market size projected to grow from around USD 3.5 billion in 2025 to approximately USD 4 billion in 2026 . This growth reflects strong enterprise demand for scalable infrastructure across sectors such as finance, retail, logistics, gaming, and government services, as digital transformation accelerates nationwide. The market is expected to maintain double-digit growth through the late 2020s.

While global hyperscalers have traditionally dominated Vietnam’s cloud sector, local cloud providers are capturing a growing share of the market. Local adoption is driven in part by regulatory emphasis on data residency and sovereignty, especially in regulated industries like finance and public services. This trend creates opportunities for cloud providers with onshore infrastructure and compliance capabilities.
Emergence of AI-Driven Cloud Demand
Artificial intelligence and GPU-accelerated computing are emerging as important drivers of cloud demand in Vietnam. Analysts forecast that ongoing uptake of AI technologies will contribute significantly to cloud market expansion, with the ecosystem potentially reaching around USD 1.5 billion by 2030, largely driven by AI workloads and enterprise digital transformation.

Key Criteria for Foreign Companies Choosing a Cloud Provider in Vietnam
For foreign companies entering Vietnam, selecting a cloud provider is not simply a technical decision. It directly affects regulatory compliance, user experience, operational efficiency, and long-term scalability. In 2026, the Vietnamese cloud landscape offers multiple options, but the evaluation framework must go beyond pricing and brand recognition.
Below are the key criteria foreign companies should consider when choosing a cloud provider in Vietnam.
Data Residency and Regulatory Alignment
Vietnam’s Cybersecurity Law and related data regulations increasingly require certain categories of user data to be stored locally, particularly in sectors such as fintech, e-commerce, telecommunications, and digital platforms. As regulators strengthen enforcement, hosting sensitive workloads entirely offshore can introduce legal and operational risk.

Foreign companies should verify whether a cloud provider operates onshore data centers in Vietnam, supports local data storage requirements, and holds internationally recognized certifications such as ISO 27001 or SOC 2. Beyond compliance, local hosting can also simplify audits, shorten approval processes, and build trust with Vietnamese partners and customers.

Why Choosing the Right Cloud Provider in Vietnam Matters in 2026
Vietnam is entering a new phase of digital growth. By 2026, the country is no longer viewed only as a manufacturing hub or an outsourcing destination. It is becoming a serious market for SaaS, AI-driven products, fintech, gaming, and regional digital platforms. For foreign companies planning to enter or expand in Vietnam, cloud infrastructure is no longer a background technical decision. It is a core part of market entry strategy.
Many global companies initially assume they can rely entirely on their existing cloud setup in Singapore, Hong Kong, or Tokyo. In practice, this approach often creates challenges. Latency becomes noticeable for real-time applications. Data residency requirements introduce legal and operational risk. Support and billing friction slow down deployment timelines. As workloads scale, these issues compound.

Rapid Market Growth and Expansion
Vietnam’s cloud computing market is expanding quickly, with market size projected to grow from around USD 3.5 billion in 2025 to approximately USD 4 billion in 2026 . This growth reflects strong enterprise demand for scalable infrastructure across sectors such as finance, retail, logistics, gaming, and government services, as digital transformation accelerates nationwide. The market is expected to maintain double-digit growth through the late 2020s.
Increasing Local Provider Presence and Data Sovereignty Trends
While global hyperscalers have traditionally dominated Vietnam’s cloud sector, local cloud providers are capturing a growing share of the market. Local adoption is driven in part by regulatory emphasis on data residency and sovereignty, especially in regulated industries like finance and public services. This trend creates opportunities for cloud providers with onshore infrastructure and compliance capabilities.

Data Residency and Regulatory Alignment

Data Residency and Regulatory Alignment
Vietnam’s Cybersecurity Law and related data regulations increasingly require certain categories of user data to be stored locally, particularly in sectors such as fintech, e-commerce, telecommunications, and digital platforms. As regulators strengthen enforcement, hosting sensitive workloads entirely offshore can introduce legal and operational risk.
Foreign companies should verify whether a cloud provider operates onshore data centers in Vietnam, supports local data storage requirements, and holds internationally recognized certifications such as ISO 27001 or SOC 2. Beyond compliance, local hosting can also simplify audits, shorten approval processes, and build trust with Vietnamese partners and customers.

Infrastructure Performance and AI Readiness
Vietnam’s digital economy is expanding rapidly, with strong growth in gaming, fintech, SaaS, and AI-driven applications. Latency-sensitive services, especially real-time platforms and AI inference workloads, can be impacted if infrastructure is hosted outside the country.
Companies should assess:
Local network latency to major Vietnamese cities
Availability of high-performance compute and GPU instances
Storage throughput for data-intensive workloads
As AI adoption increases, demand for GPU cloud infrastructure is rising. Providers that offer scalable GPU clusters and AI-optimized environments can significantly reduce training time and improve cost efficiency compared to adapting general-purpose infrastructure.
Scalability and Long-Term Cost Structure
Vietnam is often part of a broader Southeast Asia expansion strategy. Infrastructure decisions made at market entry must support long-term growth without frequent redesign.

Whether the provider supports horizontal and vertical scaling

Why is base frequency important?
When evaluating CPU performance in the cloud, many people focus on turbo frequency—the figure that reflects a CPU’s ability to boost speed for short periods. However, for modern enterprise workloads, real-world performance is more often determined by base frequency.
Workloads such as multi-hour AI model training, large-scale batch processing, continuous ETL pipelines, and complex data warehouse queries share a common trait: the CPU must operate near 100% utilization for extended periods, rather than spiking briefly during short processing bursts.
In these scenarios, turbo frequency cannot be sustained due to thermal and power limits. Under prolonged full-load conditions, CPU clock speeds gradually stabilize at the base frequency—this is the performance level the system can maintain consistently over time.

title: GreenNode delivers hyperscale-class high-performance Cloud AI platform for enterprise workloads
text:
d conditions, CPU clock speeds gradually stabilize at the base frequency—this is the performance level the system can maintain consistently over time.
Therefore, for long-running, high-intensity workloads, base frequency most accurately reflects a CPU’s real performance. Infrastructure with higher base frequencies can process more tasks per core within the same time frame, shortening job completion times and optimizing operational costs.
This is also a clear advantage of GreenNode, which uses CPU configurations with higher base clocks, making them well-suited for enterprise workloads that demand stable, sustained performance rather than short-term bursts.
Real-world impact on different workloads
Differences in basefrequency are not just technical specifications on paper—they directly affect the real-world performance of many enterprise workloads.
AI and ML training

title: GreenNode delivers hyperscale-class high-performance Cloud AI platform for enterprise workloads
text:
quency are not just technical specifications on paper—they directly affect the real-world performance of many enterprise workloads.
AI and ML training
In AI systems, the CPU is responsible for data preprocessing, feature engineering, and pipeline orchestration before data is sent to the GPU. These processes typically run continuously throughout the model training cycle. If the CPU becomes a bottleneck, GPUs can end up waiting idle, reducing the efficiency of these costly resources.
With a 2.7 GHz base frequency, the Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P enables faster and more stable CPU-side data pipeline processing, lowering the risk of bottlenecks and maintaining higher GPU utilization compared to configurations with only 2.1–2.3 GHz base frequencies.
The Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P (2.7 GHz base frequency) speeds up CPU pipelines, reduces bottlenecks, and keeps GPU utilization higher than CPUs with 2.1–2.3 GHz base frequency.
Big Data and Analytics
Data analytics systems such as Spark, Presto, ClickHouse, and BigQuery-like engines rely heavily on thread-level parallelism, memory bandwidth, and stable CPU clock speeds.

title: GreenNode delivers hyperscale-class high-performance Cloud AI platform for enterprise workloads
text:
such as Spark, Presto, ClickHouse, and BigQuery-like engines rely heavily on thread-level parallelism, memory bandwidth, and stable CPU clock speeds.
During long-running queries or jobs, CPUs in hyperscale environments often drop to base frequencies of 2.1–2.3 GHz. In contrast, configurations with a 2.7 GHz base frequency can sustain higher per-core processing performance. This results in shorter query times and higher throughput on the same number of cores, especially for large-scale data analytics tasks.
High-performance databases
OLAP and OLTP database systems run continuously under heavy workloads, where latency and per-core performance directly impact application experience. Under prolonged full-load conditions, turbo frequencies are no longer sustained, and the system depends on the CPU’s base clock.
A higher base clock helps reduce query tail latency and maintain more stable transaction throughput, particularly in systems that require fast response times and handle high transaction volumes.
Conclusion

title: GreenNode delivers hyperscale-class high-performance Cloud AI platform for enterprise workloads
text:
maintain more stable transaction throughput, particularly in systems that require fast response times and handle high transaction volumes.
Conclusion
Even when using the same 5th Gen Intel Xeon Platinum family, differences in CPU models and base clock configurations can lead to significant differences in real-world performance. Hyperscalers like Google Cloud typically optimize infrastructure for a broad mix of workloads at massive scale, while CPU configurations with higher base clocks are especially well-suited for continuous AI, Big Data, HPC, and database workloads.
As enterprises increasingly rely on long-running, high-intensity workloads, stable baseline performance becomes a key factor in both efficiency and operational cost. With a CPU platform designed to sustain real-world performance at hyperscale levels, GreenNode reinforces its position as a pioneer in cloud infrastructure innovation in Vietnam and across APAC—true to its positioning as a “High-performance AI cloud for digital-native businesses.”

With a 2.7 GHz base frequency, the Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P enables faster and more stable CPU-side data pipeline processing, lowering the risk of bottlenecks and maintaining higher GPU utilization compared to configurations with only 2.1–2.3 GHz base frequencies.
The Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P (2.7 GHz base frequency) speeds up CPU pipelines, reduces bottlenecks, and keeps GPU utilization higher than CPUs with 2.1–2.3 GHz base frequency.
Big Data and Analytics
Data analytics systems such as Spark, Presto, ClickHouse, and BigQuery-like engines rely heavily on thread-level parallelism, memory bandwidth, and stable CPU clock speeds.

title: GreenNode delivers hyperscale-class high-performance Cloud AI platform for enterprise workloads
text:
such as Spark, Presto, ClickHouse, and BigQuery-like engines rely heavily on thread-level parallelism, memory bandwidth, and stable CPU clock speeds.
During long-running queries or jobs, CPUs in hyperscale environments often drop to base frequencies of 2.1–2.3 GHz. In contrast, configurations with a 2.7 GHz base frequency can sustain higher per-core processing performance. This results in shorter query times and higher throughput on the same number of cores, especially for large-scale data analytics tasks.
High-performance databases
OLAP and OLTP database systems run continuously under heavy workloads, where latency and per-core performance directly impact application experience. Under prolonged full-load conditions, turbo frequencies are no longer sustained, and the system depends on the CPU’s base clock.
A higher base clock helps reduce query tail latency and maintain more stable transaction throughput, particularly in systems that require fast response times and handle high transaction volumes.
Conclusion

title: GreenNode delivers hyperscale-class high-performance Cloud AI platform for enterprise workloads
text:
maintain more stable transaction throughput, particularly in systems that require fast response times and handle high transaction volumes.
Conclusion
Even when using the same 5th Gen Intel Xeon Platinum family, differences in CPU models and base clock configurations can lead to significant differences in real-world performance. Hyperscalers like Google Cloud typically optimize infrastructure for a broad mix of workloads at massive scale, while CPU configurations with higher base clocks are especially well-suited for continuous AI, Big Data, HPC, and database workloads.
As enterprises increasingly rely on long-running, high-intensity workloads, stable baseline performance becomes a key factor in both efficiency and operational cost. With a CPU platform designed to sustain real-world performance at hyperscale levels, GreenNode reinforces its position as a pioneer in cloud infrastructure innovation in Vietnam and across APAC—true to its positioning as a “High-performance AI cloud for digital-native businesses.”

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
Key takeaways
Global data center CPU demand is surging as AI and cloud workloads grow, pushing the market to roughly double in size over the next decade.
New CPUs like Intel Xeon Platinum 8558P are built for cloud-native and AI workloads, and GreenNode plans to offer them so enterprises get high-performance compute without buying hardware.
The market for cloud infrastructure — particularly CPUs and high-performance processors—is emerging as one of the most critical pillars of global technology growth. With the rapid expansion of AI, machine learning, cloud services, and big data applications, demand for powerful, efficient, and flexible CPUs has surged significantly and is expected to continue growing strongly over the next decade.
Market size and growth forecast

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
ficient, and flexible CPUs has surged significantly and is expected to continue growing strongly over the next decade.
Market size and growth forecast
According to the latest report from Precedence Research , the global data center CPU market is valued at approximately USD 14.19 billion in 2025 and is projected to nearly double to around USD 28.04 billion by 2034, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of about 7.87% during the 2025–2034 period. This growth highlights the sustained demand for high-performance CPUs as cloud computing and AI workloads continue to scale.
Data Center CPU Market Size (2025–2034) (Source: Precedence Research)
Meanwhile, an independent study by Mordor Intelligence also indicates that the CPU segment within the global data center processor market (alongside GPUs, ASICs, etc.) will continue to grow strongly, driven by demand for faster data processing, higher performance, and chip architectures optimized for AI workloads.
Market Growth Drivers

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
ow strongly, driven by demand for faster data processing, higher performance, and chip architectures optimized for AI workloads.
Market Growth Drivers
The strong growth of the data center CPU and chip market is being fueled by multiple technology trends occurring in parallel—from the AI wave and cloud adoption to the rapid scaling of computing demand and ongoing innovation in processor architectures.
1. The Rise of AI & Machine Learning
CPUs not only play a central role in system orchestration, but also support complex parallel processing and integrate with accelerators such as GPUs and TPUs to handle AI/ML workloads. The rise of enterprise AI is pushing data centers to upgrade processing components to meet massive compute requirements.
2. Widespread Cloud Adoption
Many enterprises and organizations are migrating workloads to the cloud to take advantage of flexibility, scalability, and cost efficiency. This shift requires CPUs that can handle a wide range of workloads—from data storage and application hosting to AI model deployment.
3. Rapid Growth in Computing Scale

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
es CPUs that can handle a wide range of workloads—from data storage and application hosting to AI model deployment.
3. Rapid Growth in Computing Scale
With multi-core server architectures, multitasking CPUs, and support for virtualization and containerization, modern CPU designs are becoming increasingly critical to optimizing server resource utilization while maintaining high performance.
4. Architectural Innovation Trends
Beyond traditional x86 architectures, new approaches such as ARM-based designs and specialized processors are being adopted to optimize performance-per-watt, making them well suited for cloud-native environments and AI workloads.
Demand for high-performance CPUs continues to rise as enterprises simultaneously accelerate AI adoption, cloud migration, and next-generation computing architectures.
Market Challenges & Barriers
Despite being strongly driven by AI, cloud computing, and big data, the data center chip and CPU market still faces multiple structural challenges that directly impact both hardware manufacturers and enterprises deploying infrastructure.
Intense Competition Among Manufacturers

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
ructural challenges that directly impact both hardware manufacturers and enterprises deploying infrastructure.
Intense Competition Among Manufacturers
The market is seeing increasingly fierce competition among major players such as Intel, AMD, NVIDIA, ARM, along with emerging chip designers and processor architectures. Competition is no longer limited to raw performance, but extends to energy efficiency, total cost of ownership (TCO), scalability, and optimization for AI workloads. This pressure forces vendors to invest heavily in R&D and continuously innovate architectures, while making chip supply chains and manufacturing processes more complex and costly than ever.
High Costs and Supply Shortage Risks

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
ate architectures, while making chip supply chains and manufacturing processes more complex and costly than ever.
High Costs and Supply Shortage Risks
Beyond competition, the market is heavily affected by rising production costs and prolonged global chip shortages in recent years. Access to next-generation CPUs and data center chips has become increasingly challenging, especially in developing markets. As a result, enterprises must carefully evaluate long-term infrastructure investment strategies—balancing performance, cost, and hardware availability—while increasingly turning to cloud platforms that offer more stable and flexible supply compared to on-premise infrastructure investments.
The growth of data center CPUs is not only a signal of rising infrastructure demand, but also underscores that investing in powerful, flexible compute infrastructure capable of supporting AI workloads has become mission-critical for cloud providers, SaaS companies, AI-driven enterprises, and organizations undergoing digital transformation.

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
8P are built for cloud-native and AI workloads, and GreenNode plans to offer them so enterprises get high-performance compute without buying hardware.
The market for cloud infrastructure — particularly CPUs and high-performance processors—is emerging as one of the most critical pillars of global technology growth. With the rapid expansion of AI, machine learning, cloud services, and big data applications, demand for powerful, efficient, and flexible CPUs has surged significantly and is expected to continue growing strongly over the next decade.
Market size and growth forecast
According to the latest report from Precedence Research , the global data center CPU market is valued at approximately USD 14.19 billion in 2025 and is projected to nearly double to around USD 28.04 billion by 2034, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of about 7.87% during the 2025–2034 period. This growth highlights the sustained demand for high-performance CPUs as cloud computing and AI workloads continue to scale.
Data Center CPU Market Size (2025–2034) (Source: Precedence Research)

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
for high-performance CPUs as cloud computing and AI workloads continue to scale.
Data Center CPU Market Size (2025–2034) (Source: Precedence Research)
Meanwhile, an independent study by Mordor Intelligence also indicates that the CPU segment within the global data center processor market (alongside GPUs, ASICs, etc.) will continue to grow strongly, driven by demand for faster data processing, higher performance, and chip architectures optimized for AI workloads.
Market Growth Drivers
The strong growth of the data center CPU and chip market is being fueled by multiple technology trends occurring in parallel—from the AI wave and cloud adoption to the rapid scaling of computing demand and ongoing innovation in processor architectures.
1. The Rise of AI & Machine Learning
CPUs not only play a central role in system orchestration, but also support complex parallel processing and integrate with accelerators such as GPUs and TPUs to handle AI/ML workloads. The rise of enterprise AI is pushing data centers to upgrade processing components to meet massive compute requirements.
2. Widespread Cloud Adoption

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
The rise of enterprise AI is pushing data centers to upgrade processing components to meet massive compute requirements.
2. Widespread Cloud Adoption
Many enterprises and organizations are migrating workloads to the cloud to take advantage of flexibility, scalability, and cost efficiency. This shift requires CPUs that can handle a wide range of workloads—from data storage and application hosting to AI model deployment.
3. Rapid Growth in Computing Scale
With multi-core server architectures, multitasking CPUs, and support for virtualization and containerization, modern CPU designs are becoming increasingly critical to optimizing server resource utilization while maintaining high performance.
4. Architectural Innovation Trends
Beyond traditional x86 architectures, new approaches such as ARM-based designs and specialized processors are being adopted to optimize performance-per-watt, making them well suited for cloud-native environments and AI workloads.
Demand for high-performance CPUs continues to rise as enterprises simultaneously accelerate AI adoption, cloud migration, and next-generation computing architectures.
Market Challenges & Barriers

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
rise as enterprises simultaneously accelerate AI adoption, cloud migration, and next-generation computing architectures.
Market Challenges & Barriers
Despite being strongly driven by AI, cloud computing, and big data, the data center chip and CPU market still faces multiple structural challenges that directly impact both hardware manufacturers and enterprises deploying infrastructure.
Intense Competition Among Manufacturers
The market is seeing increasingly fierce competition among major players such as Intel, AMD, NVIDIA, ARM, along with emerging chip designers and processor architectures. Competition is no longer limited to raw performance, but extends to energy efficiency, total cost of ownership (TCO), scalability, and optimization for AI workloads. This pressure forces vendors to invest heavily in R&D and continuously innovate architectures, while making chip supply chains and manufacturing processes more complex and costly than ever.
High Costs and Supply Shortage Risks

title: The Cloud Infrastructure & CPU Market: Growth Drivers and Strategic Importance in the AI Era
text:
ate architectures, while making chip supply chains and manufacturing processes more complex and costly than ever.
High Costs and Supply Shortage Risks
Beyond competition, the market is heavily affected by rising production costs and prolonged global chip shortages in recent years. Access to next-generation CPUs and data center chips has become increasingly challenging, especially in developing markets. As a result, enterprises must carefully evaluate long-term infrastructure investment strategies—balancing performance, cost, and hardware availability—while increasingly turning to cloud platforms that offer more stable and flexible supply compared to on-premise infrastructure investments.
The growth of data center CPUs is not only a signal of rising infrastructure demand, but also underscores that investing in powerful, flexible compute infrastructure capable of supporting AI workloads has become mission-critical for cloud providers, SaaS companies, AI-driven enterprises, and organizations undergoing digital transformation.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
Key takeaways
AI-driven demand and a shift of capacity to HBM have triggered a RAM “supercycle,” with DRAM and NAND flash prices spiking sharply, shortages expected to last into 2026–2028, and data center costs rising across the board.
Memory demand is now led by AI data centers and cloud services, with additional pressure from 5G/edge, PCs/gaming, smartphones, automotive and IoT, making RAM a volatile, strategic resource rather than a stable line item.
GreenNode (formerly VNG Cloud) helps enterprises navigate this volatility by shifting workloads to cloud, optimizing DDR4 vs DDR5 adoption, phasing memory upgrades, and designing right-sized, scalable architectures that balance performance, cost, and RAM market risk.
Flash memory has become one of the most in-demand commodities in the technology sector, alongside DRAM, as AI-driven workloads continue to push data storage requirements higher. According to TechPowerUp , SanDisk raised its NAND flash contract prices by approximately 50% in November 2025, reflecting tightening supply–demand conditions.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
to TechPowerUp , SanDisk raised its NAND flash contract prices by approximately 50% in November 2025, reflecting tightening supply–demand conditions.
Following a pattern similar to the sharp rise in DRAM prices - up 172% year over year - the NAND flash market is expected to face comparable upward pressure. In response, several module manufacturers have temporarily suspended shipments to reassess pricing strategies and customer commitments.
Major industry players, including Transcend, Innodisk, and Apacer Technology, are among those pausing deliveries. Transcend, in particular, has suspended new quotes and outgoing deliveries starting November 7 2025 , citing the need to evaluate next steps amid ongoing market uncertainty. The company has also indicated that the NAND flash shortage is likely to persist, with prices continuing to rise before eventually stabilizing at more sustainable levels.
AI Boom: Why do data centers need more RAM now?

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
y to persist, with prices continuing to rise before eventually stabilizing at more sustainable levels.
AI Boom: Why do data centers need more RAM now?
DRAM is a foundational component of modern IT infrastructure, and this market is inherently cyclical, alternating between periods of oversupply and shortage depending on wafer capacity, manufacturing yields, and demand. After an oversupply phase in 2024, when post-pandemic demand cooled, the market entered a new cycle in 2025, with the explosion of AI and data center demand acting as the key catalyst.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
when post-pandemic demand cooled, the market entered a new cycle in 2025, with the explosion of AI and data center demand acting as the key catalyst.
The rapid expansion of large-scale AI models has pushed hyperscalers to deploy servers at an unprecedented pace, driving strong demand for high-bandwidth memory (HBM) used in GPUs and AI accelerators. For instance, AWS has quietly raised prices on its EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML by approximately 15% recently . Because HBM offers significantly higher margins, manufacturers have reallocated wafer capacity from conventional DRAM to HBM, abruptly tightening DRAM supply. As a result, the market has entered a DRAM “supercycle”, characterized by sharp price increases (with many reports indicating multi-fold year-over-year growth) and inventories falling to critically low levels.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
aracterized by sharp price increases (with many reports indicating multi-fold year-over-year growth) and inventories falling to critically low levels.
The late-2025 crisis differs from previous cycles driven by PCs or smartphones; instead, it is dominated by AI and data center demand and reflects a supply shortage caused by reallocation, rather than organic long-term demand growth. Although memory vendors maintain substantial capital investment budgets, much of this spending is prioritized for HBM and advanced process nodes, making a rapid recovery in DRAM supply difficult. This has significantly increased data center infrastructure costs, forcing enterprises and operators to reassess hardware investment strategies and seek more flexible deployment models to mitigate the risk of cost volatility.
In a nutshell, RAM shortage was triggered by a combination of explosive demand growth and structural supply constraints, including:
AI-driven demand surge: Rapid expansion of AI data centers has massively increased demand for DRAM, HBM, and LPDDR across CPUs, GPUs, and AI accelerators, pushing memory consumption far beyond historical norms.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
as massively increased demand for DRAM, HBM, and LPDDR across CPUs, GPUs, and AI accelerators, pushing memory consumption far beyond historical norms.
Shift to HBM production: Memory manufacturers have reallocated wafer capacity from conventional DRAM to higher-margin HBM for AI workloads, sharply reducing available DRAM supply.
Slow capacity expansion: New DRAM fabs require 3–5 years to build, and vendors remain cautious about overinvesting due to concerns over a potential AI demand bubble.
Intensifying competition for supply: Hyperscalers, governments, and large OEMs are securing long-term contracts and stockpiling memory, further tightening the market.
Prolonged shortage outlook: Analysts expect the current DRAM shortage cycle to persist into 2026–2028, with supply easing only as new fabs come online.
Other benchmarks conducted by PCpartpicker.com to compare the price history of PC parts reveal a much more brutal picture:
Price fluctuations of DDR4-3600 2x32GB (Source: PCPartPicker) Price fluctuations of DDR5-6000 2x16GB (Source: PCPartPicker) Price fluctuations of SSD M.2 NVME 256GB (Source: PCPartPicker)
RAM Demand Analysis

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
ker) Price fluctuations of DDR5-6000 2x16GB (Source: PCPartPicker) Price fluctuations of SSD M.2 NVME 256GB (Source: PCPartPicker)
RAM Demand Analysis
The surge in memory demand by December 2025 is multi-layered but overwhelmingly driven by AI and data center workloads:
AI Data Centers (Primary Driver): Large-scale AI training and inference clusters now allocate memory budgets comparable to GPUs. Modern AI servers consume massive amounts of HBM and DDR5, with top-tier GPUs using up to 1TB of HBM each, rapidly draining global memory supply and pushing server DRAM prices sharply higher.
Cloud & Web Services: AI inference services (e.g. ChatGPT, Bing Chat) combined with hyperscaler growth require vast server fleets, allowing cloud providers to absorb higher memory costs and further intensifying demand.
Networking & Edge Infrastructure: 5G/6G, edge computing, and next-gen networking equipment require higher memory per unit, adding incremental demand alongside AI growth.
Consumer PCs & Gaming: While no longer the main driver, PC and gaming markets are impacted by supply tightness, with DRAM prices rising significantly and retail availability shrinking.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
nger the main driver, PC and gaming markets are impacted by supply tightness, with DRAM prices rising significantly and retail availability shrinking.
Smartphones, Automotive & IoT: Adoption of LPDDR5X in premium smartphones, along with growing memory needs in automotive, VR/AR, and IoT systems, adds further pressure to an already constrained market.
Other Electronics: Laptops, smart TVs, routers, game consoles, and other DRAM-dependent devices are facing higher costs and supply delays. As DDR3/4 kits become scarce, some production runs are delayed or more expensive, with OEMs reporting memory procurement as a bottleneck in late 2025.
The table below summarizes the major manufacturers, their estimated market share as of Q2 2025, and their strategic positioning (Source: Instuition Lab ):
Major DRAM manufacturers & and their strategic focus
Implications for Your IT Planning
Migrating from on-premises infrastructure to on-cloud: Moving workloads to a cloud platform like GreenNode* helps enterprises reduce exposure to hardware price volatility while still maintaining stable performance on high-end CPU platforms.

title: RAM Price Shock 2026: The Impact of AI on Data Center Memory
text:
m like GreenNode* helps enterprises reduce exposure to hardware price volatility while still maintaining stable performance on high-end CPU platforms.
Plan for price volatility: Memory is no longer a stable cost component. For 2026, RAM should be treated as a variable cost driver, with flexibility built into budgets and infrastructure planning.
Optimize existing platforms: Where technically feasible, continuing to leverage DDR4-based environments can be a cost-effective option, avoiding premature migration to fully provisioned DDR5 systems.
Adopt phased capacity growth: Large memory upgrades should be approached in stages—starting with a practical baseline and expanding capacity as workloads, budgets, and market conditions evolve.
Stage memory-intensive workloads: For virtualization, databases, AI, and in-memory analytics, planning capacity targets (e.g. 128 GB → 256 GB → 512 GB) in phases helps maintain scalability without committing to peak configurations at unfavorable price points.

title: AI Deployment: Compliance Challenges with Data and Technology Regulations
text:
AI Deployment: Compliance Challenges with Data and Technology Regulations
AI Deployment: Compliance Challenges with Data and Technology Regulations
The emergence and rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI) are driving structural transformations across almost all sectors. In this context, data has become a “strategic fuel”, playing a central role in enterprise operations, decision-making, and business model innovation.
To keep pace with this rapid development, the legal framework governing AI-related activities in Vietnam has gradually taken shape, with its initial foundation established under the Law on Digital Technology Industry 2025 . Subsequently, on 10 December 2025, the National Assembly passed the Law on Artificial Intelligence 2025 , which will take effect from 1 March 2026, with a focus on establishing the responsibilities of relevant stakeholders throughout the research, development, and deployment of AI.

title: AI Deployment: Compliance Challenges with Data and Technology Regulations
text:
m 1 March 2026, with a focus on establishing the responsibilities of relevant stakeholders throughout the research, development, and deployment of AI.
This development presents enterprises with the challenge of reassessing their overall approach to AI. How can compliance be ensured while AI is deployed effectively? In this article, GreenNode & Data Protectify analyze the relationship between AI, deployment infrastructure, and data-related legal requirements in the current transitional period. This helps enterprises transform compliance requirements into drivers of sustainable innovation in the AI era.
Read the full white paper here: AI Deployment - Compliance Challenges with Data and Technology Regulations

title: AI Deployment: Compliance Challenges with Data and Technology Regulations
text:
In this context, data has become a “strategic fuel”, playing a central role in enterprise operations, decision-making, and business model innovation.
To keep pace with this rapid development, the legal framework governing AI-related activities in Vietnam has gradually taken shape, with its initial foundation established under the Law on Digital Technology Industry 2025 . Subsequently, on 10 December 2025, the National Assembly passed the Law on Artificial Intelligence 2025 , which will take effect from 1 March 2026, with a focus on establishing the responsibilities of relevant stakeholders throughout the research, development, and deployment of AI.
This development presents enterprises with the challenge of reassessing their overall approach to AI. How can compliance be ensured while AI is deployed effectively? In this article, GreenNode & Data Protectify analyze the relationship between AI, deployment infrastructure, and data-related legal requirements in the current transitional period. This helps enterprises transform compliance requirements into drivers of sustainable innovation in the AI era.

title: vWAF: Firewall Protecting React/NextJS Against Critical Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182
text:
vWAF: Firewall Protecting React/NextJS Against Critical Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182
Key takeaways
CVE-2025-55182 is a critical unauthenticated RCE vulnerability in React Server Components and Next.js, rated CVSS 10.0 and allowing full server compromise via malicious POST payloads.
​GreenNode’s vWAF next-generation application firewall has been proactively updated to detect and block exploit patterns targeting this vulnerability, protecting React/Next.js workloads even before code upgrades.
​Customers are strongly advised to both enable vWAF and upgrade to the patched React/Next.js versions listed in the advisory for long-term protection, while GreenNode continues to monitor and refresh firewall rules.
The security vulnerability CVE-2025-55182 directly impacts React and Next.js, enabling remote code execution without authentication. With the vWAF application firewall, systems running React/NextJS on GreenNode are proactively protected against widespread exploitation attempts.
1. Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182: Critical RCE in React Server Components

title: vWAF: Firewall Protecting React/NextJS Against Critical Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182
text:
are proactively protected against widespread exploitation attempts.
1. Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182: Critical RCE in React Server Components
On November 29, security researcher Lachlan Davidson discovered a severe vulnerability in the deserialization module used by React and Next.js. On December 3, 2025, the React team issued an official advisory under ID CVE-2025-55182. For more information, please see this link .
This vulnerability is an unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) flaw that directly affects React Server Components (RSC) — a core feature of React 19 and frameworks built on it, especially Next.js (related ID: CVE-2025-66478).
How the vulnerability works
CVE-2025-55182 originates from unsafe deserialization within the Flight protocol of React Server Components. When the application processes HTTP POST requests to Server Function endpoints, attackers can send specially crafted encoded payloads that allow arbitrary code execution on the server.
Even if your application does not implement Server Function endpoints, enabling RSC alone is enough to expose your system to attacks.

title: vWAF: Firewall Protecting React/NextJS Against Critical Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182
text:
n on the server.
Even if your application does not implement Server Function endpoints, enabling RSC alone is enough to expose your system to attacks.
According to NVD, the vulnerability received a CVSS score of 10.0 (Critical), reflecting maximum severity:
Remote exploitation.
No authentication required.
No user interaction required.
Complete compromise of confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
2. vWAF – A Proactive “Firewall Shield” Against CVE-2025-55182
Recognizing the severity of this vulnerability, GreenNode (formerly VNG Cloud) immediately updated the vWAF application firewall rules to block exploitation techniques targeting RSC.
Updated firewall rules
vWAF now detects and blocks:
POST requests containing abnormal serialized payloads
Suspicious JSON parameters or module-call behaviors associated with exploitation
Access attempts to endpoints resembling malicious server-function patterns
With these updates, all customers who have enabled vWAF are already protected—even if they have not yet upgraded React or Next.js.
3. Security Recommendation: Upgrading Remains the Most Effective Long-Term Solution

title: vWAF: Firewall Protecting React/NextJS Against Critical Security Vulnerability CVE-2025-55182
text:
ady protected—even if they have not yet upgraded React or Next.js.
3. Security Recommendation: Upgrading Remains the Most Effective Long-Term Solution
While the vWAF firewall significantly reduces exploitation risk during the critical window, upgrading to patched versions remains the safest and most comprehensive fix.

Key takeaways
GreenNode’s fully managed Kafka cluster service lets businesses handle large-scale real-time data without building complex infrastructure, so teams can focus on applications instead of cluster operations.
Managed Kafka on GreenNode simplifies deployment, scaling, monitoring, and troubleshooting, reducing long‑term infrastructure and personnel costs compared to self-managed Kafka clusters.
The service powers high‑value use cases like user activity tracking, IoT data streams, log analytics, payment processing, and real-time analytics in banking, eCommerce, telecom, logistics, healthcare, and advertising.
In the digital age, data is a company's most valuable asset. Did you know that 90% of the world's data was created in the last two years? Processing and leveraging this massive amount of data is a significant challenge for businesses.

Why Do Businesses Need Kafka?
Before delving into Kafka and how it revolutionizes data storage and processing, let's familiarize ourselves with the concept of real-time data. As the name suggests, real-time data is collected and stored as soon as it is generated. Real-time data is often used in applications that require quick responses and continuous updates, such as:

Financial markets: Tracking stock prices, exchange rates, and other market fluctuations.

Social media: Updating user statuses, comments, and messages.
Gaming: Providing smooth and real-time interactive gaming experiences.
IoT (Internet of Things): Monitoring data from connected devices, such as temperature sensors, humidity sensors, and surveillance cameras.
It's evident that real-time data is of paramount importance to businesses. Companies need this information for a wide range of applications, including recording and improving customer service, managing products, and optimizing operations. Additionally, real-time data helps monitor and maintain IT infrastructure. Recognizing these benefits, the demand for collecting and processing this type of data is increasing across all businesses worldwide.

title: 6 Powerful Applications of Kafka Cluster Managed Services

infrastructure. Recognizing these benefits, the demand for collecting and processing this type of data is increasing across all businesses worldwide.
Therefore, tools capable of collecting and storing real-time data like Kafka are increasingly being adopted by businesses in all industries. According to statistics, over 80% of Fortune 100 companies are using Kafka .
Recognizing this trend, VNG Cloud is proud to introduce the Kafka cluster managed service – a solution built on the open-source Kafka system to simplify real-time data processing for businesses.
What is Kafka Cluster Managed service?
VNG Cloud's Kafka cluster managed service is built on the open-source Apache Kafka system. Apache Kafka is a distributed streaming platform designed to handle large volumes of data in a scalable and reliable fashion. Developed in 2011 by LinkedIn and later taken over by the Apache community, Kafka is commonly used for enterprise data processing in two ways:

title: 6 Powerful Applications of Kafka Cluster Managed Services
text:
ashion. Developed in 2011 by LinkedIn and later taken over by the Apache community, Kafka is commonly used for enterprise data processing in two ways:
Building real-time streaming data pipelines and applications: Kafka acts as a central hub for collecting data from various sources and storing it in topics. This data is then distributed and processed by multiple connected applications concurrently.
Serving as a message broker: Kafka allows different applications, written in different languages and running on different platforms, to communicate easily with each other using messages. This platform also handles message traffic, ensuring that applications are not overloaded.
However, building and managing a Kafka cluster in-house requires a highly skilled technical team and a significant investment of time and effort. This is not only costly but also diverts business resources from core activities.
GreenNode's Kafka cluster managed service provides a comprehensive solution, enabling businesses to quickly deploy and manage Kafka clusters. With this service, businesses can focus on application development and maximizing data value without worrying about technical issues.

GreenNode's Kafka service not only provides a solid foundation for real-time data processing but also supports various use cases across industries, from banking and e-commerce to telecommunications and logistics. Here are some typical use cases:

User activity tracking : eCommerce businesses or online service platforms can leverage Kafka to collect and analyze user behavior on websites or mobile applications. This helps personalize the user experience, improve engagement, and increase sales.
IoT data processing : Businesses in manufacturing, telecommunications, or IoT service providers can use Kafka to collect data from millions of IoT devices. This data is then processed immediately to make business decisions, such as optimizing operational efficiency or predicting equipment failures.
System log analysis : Kafka is a powerful tool for collecting and analyzing logs from various systems. This helps businesses quickly identify errors, optimize system performance, and enhance information security.
Payment processing : For financial institutions, fast and secure payment processing is a top priority. Kafka ensures that payment transactions are continuous and uninterrupted, while also supporting the detection of suspicious or fraudulent transactions.

eCommerce : With Kafka, businesses can track customer shopping journeys, from product search to checkout, to make relevant product recommendations and enhance the online shopping experience.
Telecommunications : Telecommunications companies use Kafka to process massive amounts of data from base stations, quickly detect errors, and optimize network performance, ensuring stable operations.
Logistics : Kafka tracks the status of goods in real-time, helping to optimize the supply chain, ensure timely delivery, and minimize risks.

Advertising : In the advertising field, Kafka supports collecting and storing real-time user behavior information. Advertising companies can use this data to provide quick feedback and offer relevant advertising suggestions. title: Top Cloud Computing Trends to Watch in 2026
text:
Top Cloud Computing Trends to Watch in 2026
Key  takeaways
AI‑as‑a‑Service is becoming a core cloud offering, letting companies rent powerful AI and GPU infrastructure instead of building their own, which lowers cost and speeds up experimentation.
Hybrid and multi‑cloud are now standard strategies, with businesses mixing on‑prem, private, and multiple public clouds to avoid vendor lock‑in, improve resilience, and optimize performance and cost.
Real‑time, edge‑enhanced cloud and cloud‑powered industry platforms (for finance, healthcare, manufacturing, etc.) are emerging, plus a strong push toward greener, more energy‑efficient cloud infrastructure.
In 2026, enterprises are no longer focused solely on scalability; they are increasingly prioritizing operational cost optimization (FinOps), automation, and greater control over cloud resources. The growing adoption of cloud across industries such as finance, retail, manufacturing, media, and technology demonstrates that cloud computing is no longer optional, but a foundational pillar of digital transformation and long-term business growth.

technology demonstrates that cloud computing is no longer optional, but a foundational pillar of digital transformation and long-term business growth.

Modern architectural models such as Hybrid Cloud, Multi-Cloud, and Federated Cloud are increasingly favored to balance performance, cost efficiency, and regulatory requirements. These approaches enable organizations to distribute workloads flexibly across multiple environments while enhancing resilience and risk management.
As we move further into 2026, cloud infrastructure continues to serve as a primary driver of technological innovation and enterprise growth. Below are 10 key trends that are shaping and will continue to shape the cloud computing landscape in 2026.

Hybrid and Multi-Cloud

title: Top Cloud Computing Trends to Watch in 2026

ing AI-as-a-service via cloud platforms allows them to harness the potential of this influential and transformative technology.
Hybrid and Multi-Cloud
More than 85–90% of large enterprises worldwide are currently implementing a multi-cloud strategy, meaning they use services from two or more cloud providers. This percentage has increased significantly compared to the 2022–2023 period and is expected to remain at a high level through 2026. While providing cost and flexibility benefits, this approach also has challenges in terms of data governance and integration with legacy systems. Multi and hybrid cloud solutions (combining cloud services with on-premises infrastructure) represent sophisticated infrastructure options that will gain further traction. Organizations are increasingly turning to these models to strike a balance between security and flexibility, allowing them to selectively opt for the services that best meet their needs.
Real-Time Cloud Infrastructure

title: Top Cloud Computing Trends to Watch in 2026
text:
balance between security and flexibility, allowing them to selectively opt for the services that best meet their needs.
Real-Time Cloud Infrastructure
In 2026, organizations will increasingly prioritize harnessing real-time data to gain up-to-the-minute insights, moving away from acting on outdated and stagnant information. Simultaneously, a growing portion of consumed data will be in the form of streamed content such as movies and music from platforms like Netflix and Spotify, video data from Zoom or Teams calls, and emerging forms of streamed entertainment like cloud gaming. This shift underscores the rising demand for data storage solutions that emphasize instantaneous access, with technologies like Flash and solid-state storage devices becoming increasingly sought after by cloud users.

technologies like Flash and solid-state storage devices becoming increasingly sought after by cloud users.

antages of emerging technologies more effortlessly than ever in 2026, all thanks to the capabilities of cloud computing.
Cloud Security and Robustness
In response to the changing threat landscape of 2026, encryption, authentication, and disaster recovery are becoming increasingly sought-after functionalities within cloud computing services. The rise in frequency and severity of data thefts and breaches, fueled by the development of new AI-powered attack methods by hackers, underscores the constant vulnerability of systems accessible to humans, susceptible to social engineering attacks. Consequently, both cloud providers and customers are placing a premium on ensuring security and resilience are top priorities.

equently, both cloud providers and customers are placing a premium on ensuring security and resilience are top priorities.
Sustainable Cloud Computing
Major cloud service providers have collectively committed to achieving net-zero emissions, extending beyond their internal operations to assist customers in reducing their carbon footprints. Amazon has set a goal to reach zero emissions by 2040, while Microsoft aims to surpass this target by a decade. Additionally, Google, along with the others, has expressed its intent to derive 100 percent of the energy used in its operations from renewable sources. While the realization of these goals is yet to be seen, the trend towards more eco-friendly and environmentally responsible cloud computing is expected to gain significant momentum in 2024.
